senses.definition: Without an arles payment; without a deposit paid to secure a contract, especially in Scots law or older regional use.
examples: The sale was declared arless and therefore not binding in court.
They agreed only in principle, leaving the contract arless until the money was paid.

senses.definition: A female given name, often treated as a variant of Arlene.
examples: Arline moved to New York to study music.
My grandmother Arline was born in the 1930s.

senses.definition: The name of several towns and cities in English‑speaking countries, especially a city in Texas in the United States.
examples: They flew to Arlington, Texas, to watch the baseball game.
She grew up in Arlington and later moved abroad.

senses.definition: Short for Arlington National Cemetery, a famous U.S. military cemetery near Washington, D.C.
examples: They visited Arlington to pay their respects to fallen soldiers.
Her grandfather is buried in Arlington.

senses.definition: An early term in sailing for the orlop deck, the lowest deck on a wooden warship where cables and some stores were kept.
examples: The wounded sailors were taken down to the arloup after the battle.
Heavy ropes were stored on the arloup to keep the ship balanced.

senses.definition: The part of your body between your shoulder and your hand.
examples: She broke her arm while playing football.
He carried the baby in his arms.

senses.definition: A long, narrow part of something that sticks out from the main part, like part of a machine, chair, or large body of water.
examples: An arm of the lake reaches almost to the village.
The robot's arm can pick up very small objects.

senses.definition: A part of a large organization or group that has a particular job or area of activity.
examples: The charity has an education arm that works in rural areas.
The research arm of the company is based in Berlin.

senses.definition: To give weapons or equipment to a person or group so they can fight.
examples: The rebels were secretly armed by foreign powers.
They armed the guards with rifles.

senses.definition: To prepare something, especially a weapon or device, so that it is ready to work or explode.
examples: The soldiers armed the bomb and left the area.
You must arm the alarm system before you go out.

senses.definition: A large group of warships moving together.
examples: An armada of ships appeared on the horizon.
The country sent an armada to protect its coast.

senses.definition: A very large group of vehicles, aircraft, or other things moving together, used humorously or for effect.
examples: An armada of tour buses blocked the narrow street.
They sent an armada of delivery vans on Black Friday.

senses.definition: Plural of armada: large groups of warships or large groups of vehicles or things moving together.
examples: Several armadas gathered near the bay during the conflict.
Armadas of fishing boats left the harbor before dawn.

senses.definition: A small aquatic crustacean of the genus Armadilla, related to woodlice and pill bugs.
examples: The biologist collected an armadilla from the stream bed.
Armadilla species are important decomposers in some freshwater habitats.

senses.definition: A family of woodlice that can roll into a ball when threatened.
examples: Armadillididae includes many species known as pill bugs.
Members of Armadillididae curl into a ball for protection.

senses.definition: A genus of woodlice that can roll into a tight ball, often called pill bugs or roly‑polies.
examples: Armadillidium vulgare is common in gardens worldwide.
Children like to play with Armadillidium, watching it roll into a ball.

senses.definition: A small American mammal with a hard shell made of bony plates, which can curl up to protect itself.
examples: We saw an armadillo crossing the road at night.
The armadillo dug a hole near the fence.

senses.definition: Plural of armadillo: small mammals with a hard shell of bony plates.
examples: Armadillos are common in some parts of the southern United States.
The zoo keeps several armadillos in a warm enclosure.

senses.definition: An old or rare spelling of armada, meaning a large fleet of warships.
examples: The chronicles tell of an armado sent against the island.
An armado of great ships sailed from the harbor at dawn.

senses.definition: In the Bible, the last great battle between good and evil at the end of the world.
examples: Some religious groups believe Armageddon is near.
The prophecy describes a final war called Armageddon.

senses.definition: A terrible and complete destruction of the world or of human life, used figuratively.
examples: Scientists warn that nuclear war could mean environmental Armageddon.
For the small town, the factory closing felt like economic Armageddon.

senses.definition: A person who strongly believes that Armageddon or a final world‑ending disaster is coming soon.
examples: He is known as an armageddonist who predicts the end of the world every few years.
Armageddonists often interpret natural disasters as signs of the coming judgment.

senses.definition: A strong French brandy made in the Armagnac region of southwest France.
examples: He ordered a glass of Armagnac after dinner.
The chef added a splash of Armagnac to the sauce.

senses.definition: Bottles or types of Armagnac brandy, treated as a countable noun.
examples: The shop specializes in rare Armagnacs.
He keeps several old Armagnacs in his cellar.

senses.definition: The weapons and military equipment that a country or group has.
examples: The ship's armament includes modern missiles and guns.
The report compared the armament of the two rival states.

senses.definition: The process of building up weapons and military forces.
examples: The treaty aims to limit further armament in the region.
Rapid armament worried neighboring countries.

senses.definition: Plural of armamentarium: the complete set of equipment, medicines, or techniques available for a particular kind of work, especially in medicine.
examples: Modern armamentaria give doctors many ways to treat the same disease.
Different surgical armamentaria are used in heart operations.

senses.definition: The full range of equipment, medicines, or techniques that a professional can use, especially in medicine.
examples: Antibiotics are an important part of the doctor's armamentarium.
The psychologist added new tools to her therapeutic armamentarium.

senses.definition: Plural of armamentarium: full sets of professional tools, especially in medicine.
examples: New drugs have greatly expanded our therapeutic armamentariums.
Different hospitals have different surgical armamentariums.

senses.definition: Relating to armaments or to a collection of tools and equipment.
examples: The armamentary section of the museum displays old weapons.
He wrote an armamentary handbook for young surgeons.

senses.definition: All the weapons and military equipment that a country has.
examples: Global spending on armaments has risen in recent years.
The treaty requires both sides to reduce their armaments.

senses.definition: A rare mineral that is a manganese arsenite, usually found as black crystals.
examples: Armangite occurs as small black rhombohedral crystals.
The sample contained traces of armangite and other arsenites.

senses.definition: Plural of armarium: cupboards or cabinets used for storage, especially in churches or libraries.
examples: Ancient manuscripts were kept in wooden armaria along the walls.
The stone armaria in the monastery still hold old vestments.

senses.definition: In a monastery, the monk responsible for the book cupboard or library.
examples: The armarian carefully recorded every volume that entered the library.
As armarian, he spent long hours among the shelves of manuscripts.

senses.definition: Plural of armary: cupboards or recesses in a church or monastery used for storing books or vestments.
examples: The stone armaries in the cloister once held precious books.
They discovered hidden armaries behind the wooden panels.

senses.definition: A small cupboard or cabinet, especially for books, in older or Latin‑influenced usage.
examples: Each student was given an armariolum for their personal books.
The priest unlocked the tiny armariolum by the altar.

senses.definition: A cupboard, closet, or chest used for storage, especially in churches for vestments or in houses for valuables.
examples: The silverware was locked in a small armarium in the dining room.
In medieval churches, the armarium often stood near the altar.

senses.definition: A cupboard or recessed space in a church or monastery wall used for storing books, vestments, or other items.
examples: The old armary beside the choir once held liturgical books.
They restored the carved doors of the medieval armary.

senses.definition: Used in scientific Latin names to mean armed or bearing spines or sharp parts.
examples: The species name armata indicates that the plant has sharp thorns.
Several beetles with the epithet armata have strong defensive spines.

senses.definition: In Ottoman‑ruled Greece, members of local Christian militias who were given authority to keep order in certain mountain areas.
examples: The armatoles guarded the passes and collected taxes for the Ottoman authorities.
Some armatoles later joined the Greek War of Independence.

senses.definition: Plural of armatolos, another form of armatoles: local Christian militiamen under Ottoman rule in Greece.
examples: The armatoli controlled remote mountain regions.
Many armatoli took advantage of their position to gain local power.

senses.definition: The part of an electric machine, such as a motor or generator, in which current is produced or that carries current.
examples: The engineer examined the armature for signs of damage.
A fault in the armature caused the motor to stop.

senses.definition: A supporting frame or skeleton inside a sculpture or model.
examples: The artist built a wire armature before adding clay.
Using a strong armature helps large sculptures keep their shape.

senses.definition: Having an armature or provided with a protective or supporting inner frame.
examples: The armatured sculpture could be moved safely without breaking.
Armatured coils improve the performance of the motor.

senses.definition: Plural of armature: central parts of electric machines or internal supporting frames in sculptures.
examples: The gallery displayed several clay figures over wire armatures.
The workshop produced armatures for different sizes of motors.

senses.definition: The process of providing an armature, especially in electric machines or sculptures.
examples: Proper armaturing is essential for high‑performance motors.
The course included a section on armaturing for large clay figures.

senses.definition: A band worn around the upper arm, often to show a role, support a cause, or show respect for someone who has died.
examples: The players wore black armbands after the former coach died.
Each volunteer wore a brightly colored armband.

senses.definition: In British English, an inflatable band worn around the upper arm to help someone float when learning to swim.
examples: The child put on her armbands before getting into the pool.
Inflatable armbands can help beginners feel more confident in the water.

senses.definition: Plural of armband: bands worn around the arm for identification, symbolism, or help with swimming.
examples: The team came onto the field wearing black armbands.
She packed her son's armbands for the holiday.

senses.definition: A comfortable chair with sides to support your arms.
examples: He sat in the armchair and read the newspaper.
There is a soft armchair in the corner of the room.

senses.definition: Used to describe someone who talks or writes about a subject without doing it in real life, often as a criticism.
examples: He is just an armchair critic who never makes anything himself.
Many armchair experts comment online about the war.

senses.definition: Plural of armchair: comfortable chairs with sides to support your arms.
examples: Two armchairs faced the fireplace.
The hotel lobby has soft sofas and armchairs.

senses.definition: Carrying weapons, such as guns.
examples: Armed guards stood at every entrance.
The police arrested two armed men near the bank.

senses.definition: Equipped with tools, information, or qualities that give you an advantage.
examples: Armed with fresh data, she challenged the manager's decision.
He went into the meeting armed with a detailed report.

senses.definition: A country in the South Caucasus region of Eurasia, between Europe and Asia.
examples: Armenia declared independence in 1991.
They spent a week traveling around Armenia.

senses.definition: Relating to or resembling apricots.
examples: The wine had a sweet, armeniaceous aroma.
An armeniaceous scent filled the kitchen as the fruit cooked.

senses.definition: A person from Armenia or whose family is from Armenia.
examples: Many Armenians live in large cities around the world.
She is an Armenian who grew up in France.

senses.definition: Relating to Armenia, its people, or its language.
examples: She enjoys Armenian music and traditional dances.
He is studying Armenian history at university.

senses.definition: Plural of Armenian: people from Armenia or of Armenian origin.
examples: Armenians have a long history and rich cultural traditions.
Many Armenians emigrated in the early twentieth century.
